query: Angular structural vs attribute directives interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No single-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this exact Angular prompt; the available Angular basics coverage is broader than the question-specific comparison needed here.

competitors.label: Angular Structural Directives Guide
competitors.selectionReason: The official structural-directives guide is the canonical source for embedded views, microsyntax, and shorthand expansion into ng-template.
competitors.winReason: The official guide is authoritative on structural directives, but our page is more interview-ready because it now pairs the tree-versus-host distinction with a same-UI before-and-after example and the ng-container follow-up.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on microsyntax, ng-template expansion, and how structural directives operate on embedded views.
theirGaps: It is less directly organized around the side-by-side structural-versus-attribute comparison that interviewers usually want.

followUpConfusion: Choose an attribute directive when the requirement is keep the same host element, but change styling, attributes, or listeners.
Only one structural shorthand can own a host element; reach for ng-container when you need multiple structural layers.

realWorldUseCases: Angular.dev explains structural directives well, so the practical framing stays close.
actionableExamples: The official guide is strong on microsyntax, but our page now gives the more interview-ready side-by-side example for destroyed subtree versus same-instance host changes.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the likely follow-up: structural directives change the tree, attribute directives keep the tree and change the host.

realWorldUseCases: Keep one bug example where a team expected visibility toggling but accidentally destroyed child state.

competitors.label: Telerik Practical Guide to Angular Data Binding and Directives
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a practical competitor because it covers directives together with the data-binding context that makes attribute directives useful.
competitors.winReason: Telerik is broad on directives, but our page now answers this exact comparison more directly with a stronger tree-destruction-versus-host-change example and the one-star-per-host rule.
theirStrengths: It gives practical binding and directive examples that are easy to map to real templates.
theirGaps: It is broader than our prompt and spends less time on the embedded-view mental model behind structural directives.

followUpConfusion: Choose an attribute directive when the requirement is keep the same host element, but change styling, attributes, or listeners.
Only one structural shorthand can own a host element; reach for ng-container when you need multiple structural layers.

realWorldUseCases: Telerik keeps directives tied to practical template usage, so the use-case comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: Telerik offers broader directive examples, but our page now gives the tighter same-UI before/after example for tree destruction versus host-only changes.
followUpConfusion: Our page is now more explicit on the exact follow-up: what the star changes, when the same host should stay alive, and why ng-container is the fix for multiple structural layers.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the tree-versus-host distinction explicit so the answer does not drift into a generic directive overview.

competitors.label: Swiftorial Structural Directives
competitors.selectionReason: This is a direct tutorial competitor for structural directives and their DOM-changing behavior.
competitors.winReason: Swiftorial explains structural directives clearly, but our page is stronger on the practical consequences interviewers care about: destroyed child state, preserved host instances, and when ng-container becomes necessary.
theirStrengths: It stays directly on structural directives with simple ngIf/ngFor examples.
theirGaps: It does not contrast structural directives against attribute directives through lifecycle and state-reset consequences.

realWorldUseCases: Structural directives decide whether a block of template exists at all
Swiftorial explains that structural directives add or remove elements, but our page is stronger on the practical consequences of tree changes versus host-only changes.
actionableExamples: Its examples are concrete, but our page now gives the stronger same-UI before/after example for destroyed subtree versus preserved host instance.
followUpConfusion: It is lighter on the senior follow-up about ng-template sugar and attribute-directive boundaries.
Keep the one-structural-shorthand-per-host rule visible so the ng-container follow-up stays easy to recall.
